headline: How Airbnb uses S3 as the backbone of its entire listing platform
why: Airbnb has 150 million+ photos of 7 million properties. Storing, serving, and versioning all of this on traditional servers would be cost-prohibitive and operationally nightmarish. S3 was the obvious choice.

topics.usage: Each listing photo is an object with a unique key (e.g. listings/12345/photo-1.jpg). No filesystem, no hierarchy — just bucket + key. Airbnb can store 150M photos without managing a single disk.
Airbnb created region-specific buckets: airbnb-photos-us-east-1, airbnb-photos-eu-west-1. Users in Europe load photos from the EU bucket — lower latency, GDPR compliance.
Hosts can update listing photos. Airbnb keeps previous versions in S3 Versioning — if a host accidentally deletes a photo, support can restore it. Also used for A/B testing different photo crops.

Raw uploaded photos go to a private bucket (Block Public Access ON). Airbnb's processing pipeline resizes them, watermarks them, then copies approved photos to the public CDN bucket. Encryption at rest (SSE-S3) on both.

diagram:
graph TD
    Host([🏠 Host uploads photo]) --> Private[Private S3 Bucket\nBlock Public Access ON\nSSE-S3 encrypted]
    Private --> Pipeline[Processing Pipeline\nResize + Watermark]
    Pipeline --> Public[Public CDN Bucket\nCloudFront distribution]
    Public --> User([👤 Guest views listing])
    Private --> Versions[Versioning enabled\nRestore deleted photos]
    style Host fill:#1e293b,color:#94a3b8
    style User fill:#1e293b,color:#94a3b8
    style Private fill:#450a0a,color:#fca5a5
    style Pipeline fill:#7c3aed,color:#fff
    style Public fill:#1e3a5f,color:#dbeafe
    style Versions fill:#1a3320,color:#86efac
takeaway: Airbnb's S3 design separates raw uploads (private) from public-facing assets, uses versioning for recoverability, and uses regional buckets for compliance + latency. Each S3 feature we studied has a direct purpose here.
